items.note: 日焼け (hiyake) = sunburn, literally 'sun-scorching'. 止め (dome) = stopper, from 止める (to stop/prevent). So 日焼け止め = 'sunburn stopper'. Japan has strong UV in summer — convenience stores sell it everywhere. Useful phrase: 「日焼け止めを塗ったほうがいいですよ」 = You should put on sunscreen! 日 = sun, 焼け = burnt (yake, from 焼ける). 止める appears in other compounds: 血止め (chidomé) = styptic (blood stopper), 痛み止め (itamidome) = painkiller.

傘 (kasa) is one kanji for both umbrella AND parasol. Japan is famously rainy — convenience stores always sell cheap clear plastic umbrellas (ビニール傘, biniiru gasa) for around ¥500. 「傘を忘れてしまいました！」 = I unfortunately forgot my umbrella! The kanji 傘 resembles an umbrella from above: the top part = canopy, the bottom part = handle and ribs. Appears in: 日傘 (higasa) = parasol (sun-umbrella), 相合傘 (aiaikasa) = two people sharing an umbrella — a romantic sign in Japan!

ゴミ = garbage/rubbish. 箱 (hako) = box. IMPORTANT: Japan has very few public trash cans — a legacy of a 1995 sarin gas attack in Tokyo subways (suspicious packages = security risk). Convenience stores have bins for their own food waste, but streets are nearly bin-less. The custom: 「ゴミは持ち帰りましょう」 = Please take your rubbish home with you. Recycling is also strict: cans/bottles/burnable/non-burnable all sorted separately. Ask: 「このゴミはどこに捨てますか？」 = Where do I throw away this rubbish?

温める (atatameru) = to warm/heat. 直す (naosu) = to fix/redo. 温め直す = 'warm it again properly'. 「料理を温め直してください」 = Please reheat the food. A common request in Japanese restaurants and izakayas if your dish goes cold. Phrase at a restaurant: 「少し冷めてしまったので、温め直していただけますか？」 = The food got a bit cold — could you please reheat it? In konbini: 「温めますか？」 = Shall I heat it up? (standard question at the register when buying food).

items.script: お水をいただけますか
items.kana: おみずをいただけますか
english: could I have some water, please?
items.note: お水 (omizu) = water (polite: お prefix added). いただけますか = 'could I humbly receive?'. This is the most polite way to request something at a restaurant. In Japan, free water (お冷 or お水) is almost always provided in restaurants — but asking this way shows excellent manners. Three levels: ①「水ください」 (direct) ②「お水をもらえますか？」 (polite) ③「お水をいただけますか？」 (very polite). お冷 (ohiya) = cold water in a restaurant context (different word). 炭酸水 (tansansui) = sparkling water.

items.script: お好みはどちらですか
items.kana: おこのみはどちらですか
items.note: お好み (okonomi) = preference/taste, from 好む (konomu) = to prefer/like. どちら = which of two (polite; casual: どっち). Full phrase: 「どちらがお好みですか？」 = Which do you prefer? (lit. which is your preference?). Also: 「お好みに合わせます」 = We'll adjust to your preference. The classic dish お好み焼き (okonomiyaki) = 'cook-what-you-like pancake' uses the same 好み. Casual: 「どっちがいい？」 = Which do you want?

薬 (kusuri/yaku) = medicine/drug. 局 (kyoku) = bureau/office. 薬局 = the official pharmacy (licensed pharmacist on-site). You may also see: ドラッグストア (druggu sutoa) = drugstore (less formal, but same thing in Japan). Large chains: マツキヨ (Matsukiyoshi), スギ薬局, サンドラッグ. Very useful for: 風邪薬 (kazegusuri) = cold medicine, 胃薬 (igusuri) = stomach medicine, 解熱剤 (kainetsuzai) = fever reducer. Ask: 「この薬は英語の説明がありますか？」 = Does this medicine have English instructions?

One of the trickier medical words to remember! 絆 (ban) = bond/tie. 創 (sou) = wound. 膏 (kou) = ointment/paste. Literally 'wound-binding paste'. In everyday speech, Japanese people often say the brand name カットバン (Kattobando) or simply バンドエイド (Band-Aid). Ask at pharmacy: 「絆創膏はありますか？」 = Do you have any plasters? Size: Lサイズ (L saizu) = large size, small (S). Japan has adorable character-branded band-aids (Hello Kitty, etc.) that are popular souvenirs.

虫 (mushi) = insect/bug. 除け (yoke) = repelling (from 除ける, yokeru = to avoid/ward off). スプレー = spray. 虫除け alone = bug repellent (any form). Japan has mosquitoes (蚊, ka) in summer and in wooded/temple areas. Useful especially for Kyoto garden walks and outdoor festivals. Also: 蚊取り線香 (katori senko) = mosquito-repelling incense coil (iconic Japanese summer item!). The kanji 虫 appears in many words: 虫歯 (mushiba) = cavity (lit. 'tooth bug'), 毛虫 (kemushi) = caterpillar (lit. 'hairy bug').

現 (gen) = present/actual. 金 (kin/kane) = gold/money. 現金 = 'actual money' = cash. Japan is famously cash-heavy — many small restaurants, street food stalls, and temples are cash-only. 「現金のみです」 = Cash only. ATMs: You can find international ATMs at コンビニ (7-Eleven, Lawson's FamilyMart) and Japan Post. 「クレジットカードは使えますか？」 = Can I use a credit card? 「現金で払います」 = I'll pay in cash. The opposite: カード払い (kaado barai) = card payment, キャッシュレス (kyasshu resu) = cashless.

両 (ryou) = both/pair/quantity. 替える (kaeru) = to exchange. 両替 = exchanging currency. 「両替はどこでできますか？」 = Where can I exchange currency? Banks and airport currency booths (両替所, ryougaejo) offer this. Also: 両替機 (ryougaeki) = change machine (for coins, common at vending machines and arcades). Note: 「お釣り」 (otsuri) = change (money back after paying). 「1000円札を100円玉に両替してください」 = Please change a 1000 yen note into 100 yen coins.

充電 (juuden) = charging (a battery). 器 (ki) = device/tool. 充電器 = charging device. 「充電器を忘れてしまいました」 = I forgot my charger! 「コンセントを使ってもいいですか？」 = May I use the outlet? Japan uses Type A plugs (two flat pins, same as the US) at 100V/50-60Hz — phones and laptops usually accept 100-240V so they work fine. For cables: USBケーブル (yuu-esu-bii keeburu) = USB cable. 「スマホの充電が切れそうです」 = My phone is about to run out of battery.

items.script: アレルギーがあります
items.kana: あれるぎーがあります
items.note: アレルギー (arerugii) = allergy (from German 'Allergie'). 「〜アレルギーがあります」 = I have an allergy to ~. Specify: エビアレルギー (ebi = shrimp), ナッツアレルギー (nattsu = nuts), 卵アレルギー (tamago = egg). Soy (大豆, daizu) and wheat (小麦, komugi) are very common in Japanese food (soy sauce = 醤油 has both). A card to carry: アレルギーカード (arerugii kaado) = allergy card with your specific restrictions in Japanese. Show at restaurants: 「これには〜が入っていますか？」 = Does this contain ~?

items.kana: ぬきでおねがいします
items.note: 抜き (nuki) = 'without', from 抜く (nuku) = to remove/pull out. 「ネギ抜きでお願いします」 = Without spring onion, please. 「シソ抜きでお願いします」 = Without shiso, please. Very useful at sushi restaurants where toppings can be removed. Also: 「辛くしないでください」 = Please don't make it spicy (works for curry). 「肉抜き」 = without meat. Note: for complex dietary restrictions, showing a written card (辞書カード) in Japanese is more reliable. 抜き appears in: サービス抜き = without service/tip (Japan has no tipping culture anyway!).

items.script: 写真を撮ってもいいですか
items.kana: しゃしんをとってもいいですか
romaji: shashin wo totte mo ii desu ka
english: is it ok to take a photo?
items.note: 写真 (shashin) = photograph. 撮って (totte) = taking (from 撮る, toru = to take a photo). てもいいですか = is it okay if I ~? This is essential! Many Japanese temples, museums, and restaurants prohibit photos or have strict rules. 撮影禁止 (satsuei kinshi) = no photography. When in doubt, always ask first — it's good manners. 「〜さん、写真を一緒に撮ってもいいですか？」 = Is it OK if I take a photo together with you? (for selfies with someone). 「私のカメラで撮っていただけますか？」 = Could you take a photo for me?

The most fundamental question for curious travellers! これ = this (near me). は = topic marker. 何 (nan/nani) = what. Before a consonant sound, 何 = nani; before です, だ, or の, it becomes nan. 「あれは何ですか？」 = What is that (over there)? 「この料理の名前は何ですか？」 = What is the name of this dish? At markets: 「これはどんな食べ物ですか？」 = What kind of food is this? 「どうやって食べますか？」 = How do you eat this? Points system: これ/それ/あれ/どれ = this/that/that-over-there/which.

道 (michi) = road/path/way. に = location particle. 迷う (mayou) = to get lost / to be uncertain. 迷いました = past form (I got lost). Japanese addresses are notoriously confusing — even locals use apps. Don't be shy to ask: 「すみません、道に迷いました。〜はどこですか？」 = Excuse me, I'm lost. Where is ~? 「地図を見せていただけますか？」 = Could you show me a map? 迷 also appears in: 迷子 (maigo) = lost child, 迷惑 (meiwaku) = nuisance/trouble, 迷路 (meiro) = maze.

手洗い (tearai) = 'hand-washing place'. お is a polite prefix. The most polite word for toilet in Japanese. Others: トイレ (toire) = toilet (most common/casual), 化粧室 (keshoushitsu) = powder room (very formal, in department stores). 「お手洗いはどこですか？」 = Where is the toilet? Japan is famous for its toilets: 温水洗浄便座 (onsui senjo benza) = heated seat + bidet = common everywhere. Buttons: 大 (dai) = large flush, 小 (shou) = small flush, 流す (nagasu) = flush, 音姫 (otohime) = sound princess (plays flushing sound for privacy).

袋 (fukuro) = bag/sack. 要る (iru) = to need (not 居る = to exist!). 「袋は要りますか？」 = Do you need a bag? (shop assistant asks you). Response: 「要りません、大丈夫です」 = No need, I'm fine. OR 「はい、お願いします」 = Yes please. Japan now charges for plastic bags (プラスチック袋, purasuchikku bukuro) at most shops since a 2020 law. エコバッグ (eko baggu) = reusable bag (eco-bag). Having your own saves coins and is environmentally responsible. A tiny fold-up eco-bag is a great travel item.

〜は (topic marker) + どこ (where) + で (location of action) + 買えます (can buy, potential form of 買う) + か (question). 「日焼け止めはどこで買えますか？」 = Where can I buy sunscreen? 「傘はどこで買えますか？」 = Where can I buy an umbrella? 買えます = potential/can-buy form. The で here marks where the buying happens (not に which is destination). Often the answer involves: コンビニ (konbini) = convenience store, ドラッグストア = drugstore, デパート (depaato) = department store, 百均 (hyakkin) = 100-yen shop (like Daiso).
